blocks:
Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْغَـٰشِيَةِ (١)
Did you receive the account of the Enveloper? (1)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَـٰشِعَةٌ (٢)
Some faces on that day will be humbled, (2)
عَامِلَةٌ نَّاصِبَةٌ (٣)
wrought-up and weary: (3)
تَصْلَىٰ نَارًا حَامِيَةً (٤)
they will enter a scorching fire (4)
تُسْقَىٰ مِنْ عَيْنٍ ءَانِيَةٍ (٥)
and made to drink from a boiling spring. (5)
لَّيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِن ضَرِيعٍ (٦)
They will have no food except cactus, (6)
لَّا يُسْمِنُ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِن جُوعٍ (٧)
neither nourishing, nor of avail against hunger. (7)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Ghashiyah"

This Surah, which is among the Meccan Surahs, primarily revolves around three axes:

The "First Axis" is the discussion of "Resurrection," especially the painful punishments of the criminals and the soul-stirring rewards of the believers.

The "Second Axis" is the discussion of "Monotheism" (Tawhid), which is expressed by referring to the creation of the sky, the mountains, and the earth, directing human attention to these three mysterious subjects.

And the "Third Axis" is the discussion of "Prophethood" and a glimpse into the duties of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله; overall, it pursues the objectives of Meccan Surahs, which is to strengthen the foundations of faith and belief.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: «من قرأها حاسبه اللَّه حسابا يسيرا» "Whoever recites it, Allah will make his accounting easy on the Day of Resurrection." [^1] And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever persists in reciting this Surah in his obligatory or recommended prayers, Allah will place him under the shroud of His mercy in this world and the Hereafter, and will grant him security from the punishment of the Fire on the Day of Resurrection." [^2] Certainly, all these rewards and virtues are attained if the recitation becomes a motivation for reflection and action.

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 477.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ الْغاشِيَةِ (1) وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خاشِعَةٌ (2) عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ (3) تَصْلى‌ ناراً حامِيَةً (4) تُسْقى‌ مِنْ عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ (5) لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلاَّ مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ (6) لا يُسْمِنُ وَ لا يُغْنِي مِنْ جُوعٍ (7)

Commentary

The Exhausted and Deprived!

At the beginning of this Surah, we encounter a new name for the Resurrection, which is "Al-Ghashiyah." It says: "Has there come to you the report of the Overwhelming [Event]?" (هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ الْغاشِيَةِ). "Ghashiyah" is derived from the root "ghishawah," meaning to cover. This name is chosen for the Resurrection because its terrifying events suddenly cover everyone under their shroud.

Some have said it is because the first and the last of creation are gathered on that day for accounting. Others have said it refers to the Fire that covers the faces of the disbelievers and criminals. However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

It appears that the addressee in this verse is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and mentioning this sentence in the form of a question to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is to express the greatness and importance of that momentous day. Some have also suggested that the addressee in this verse is every human being, but this meaning seems unlikely.

Then, describing the state of the criminals, it says: "[Some] faces, that Day, will be humbled" (وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ خاشِعَةٌ). Humiliation and fear of the punishment and the great penalties of that day have encompassed their entire being. Since the spiritual states of a human are reflected in their face more than anywhere else, it refers to the fear, humiliation, and terror that covers their entire countenance. Some have also said: "faces" here refers to the leaders and chiefs of disbelief who will be plunged into deep humiliation, but the first meaning seems more fitting.

Then it adds: "Laboring [and] exhausted" (عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ). They exerted much effort and struggle in the life of this world, but in reality, they gain nothing but exhaustion. They have no accepted deeds in the presence of Allah, nor can they take anything from all the wealth they accumulated, nor do they leave behind a good name or righteous children. They are the exhausted and wretched laborers, and how eloquent is the expression "عامِلَةٌ ناصِبَةٌ" regarding them.

Some have said in the interpretation of this phrase: it means they work in this world but bear exhaustion and suffering in the Hereafter! Others have said: the criminals are forced to perform grueling and back-breaking tasks in Hell to suffer more punishment. However, the first interpretation seems the most correct among these three.

Finally, these exhausted and futile laborers "enter a burning Fire" (تَصْلى‌ ناراً حامِيَةً). "Tasla" is from the root "sala" (on the weight of nafy), meaning to enter the fire, remain there, and be burned by it. [^1]

But their punishment does not end there; rather, when they become thirsty due to the heat of the fire, "They are given to drink from a boiling spring" (تُسْقى‌ مِنْ عَيْنٍ آنِيَةٍ). "Aniyah" is the feminine of "ani" from the root "ana" (on the weight of hali), meaning to delay, and it is said to describe the arrival of the time of something. Here, it means boiling water whose heat has reached its ultimate degree.

In verse 29 of Surah Al-Kahf, we read: وَ إِنْ يَسْتَغِيثُوا يُغاثُوا بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ يَشْوِي الْوُجُوهَ بِئْسَ الشَّرابُ وَ ساءَتْ مُرْتَفَقاً: "And if they call for relief, they will be relieved with water like murky oil, which scalds [their] faces. Wretched is the drink, and evil is the resting place."

In the following verse, regarding their food when they become hungry, it adds: "For them there will be no food except from a poisonous, thorny plant" (لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ). Regarding what "Dari'" is, commentators have various interpretations:

Some have said: it is a type of thorn that clings to the ground; when it is fresh, the Quraysh called it "shibraq," and when it dried, they called it "dari'." It is a poisonous plant that no animal or beast approaches. [^1] "Khalil," one of the lexicographers, says: "Dari' is a foul-smelling green plant that is cast out from the sea."

Ibn Abbas said: "It is a tree of fire which, if it were in this world, would burn the earth and everything on it!" However, in a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Dari' is something in the fire of Hell, resembling thorns, more bitter than aloes, more foul-smelling than a carcass, and hotter than fire; Allah has named it Dari'." (الضريع شى‌ء يكون فى النار يشبه الشوك، اشد مرارة من الصبر، و انتن من الجيفة، و احر من النار، سماه اللَّه ضريعا). Some have also said: "Dari'" is a humiliating food that the people of Hell cry out (tadarru') to Allah to be saved from. [^2] (Let us not forget that the root "dara'" means weakness, humiliation, and submission). [^3] These interpretations are not contradictory and may be combined in the meaning of this word.

Then it adds: "Which neither nourishes nor avails against hunger" (لا يُسْمِنُ وَ لا يُغْنِي مِنْ جُوعٍ).

[^1]: "صلى بالنار" i.e., he remained in it and was burned by it. [^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7119. [^2]: Ibid, p. 7120. [^3]: We had another discussion regarding the food of the people of Hell, which the Quran sometimes refers to as "Dari'," sometimes as "Zaqqum," and sometimes as "Ghislin," and the difference between these expressions in Vol. 24 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 471, under verse 36 of Surah Al-Haqqah.

Certainly, such food is not for strengthening the body or quenching hunger; it is a food that chokes the throat, which is itself a type of punishment, as we read in verse 13 of Surah Al-Muzzammil: وَ طَعاماً ذا غُصَّةٍ وَ عَذاباً أَلِيماً: "And food that chokes and a painful punishment!" Those who in this world provided various delicious, fatty, and sweet foods through oppression and transgression against the rights of others, and those who did not allow the deprived to consume anything but choking and unpleasant food, must have a food there that becomes their "painful punishment."

Of course, as we have said many times, neither the heavenly blessings nor the hellish punishments can be fully described for us, the prisoners of the world's jail; these are all indications and only shadows that we see from afar.

Ayahs

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاعِمَةٌ (٨)
Some faces on that day will be joyous, (8)
لِّسَعْيِهَا رَاضِيَةٌ (٩)
pleased with their endeavour, (9)
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ (١٠)
in a lofty garden, (10)
لَّا تَسْمَعُ فِيهَا لَـٰغِيَةً (١١)
where they will not hear any vain talk. (11)
فِيهَا عَيْنٌ جَارِيَةٌ (١٢)
In it is a flowing spring (12)
فِيهَا سُرُرٌ مَّرْفُوعَةٌ (١٣)
and raised couches, (13)
وَأَكْوَابٌ مَّوْضُوعَةٌ (١٤)
with goblets set, (14)
وَنَمَارِقُ مَصْفُوفَةٌ (١٥)
and cushions laid out in an array, (15)
وَزَرَابِىُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ (١٦)
and carpets spread out. (16)

Commentary

A Vision of the Soul-Nurturing Blessings of Paradise

Following the description in the preceding verses regarding the state of the criminals and evildoers in the next world and the torments of Hell, these verses proceed to describe the state of the righteous believers and the incomparable blessings of Paradise. This is done to blend "Wrath" with "Mercy" and to accompany "Warning" with "Glad Tidings."

He says: "Some faces on that Day will be radiant, fresh, and overwhelmed with joy" (وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناعِمَةٌ).

This is in contrast to the faces of the evildoers mentioned in the previous verses, which are drowned in humiliation and grief.

"Nā‘imah" (ناعمة) is derived from the root "Ni‘mah" (نعمة). Here, it refers to faces that are immersed in blessings, appearing fresh, vibrant, joyful, and luminous. This is similar to what is stated in Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 24, describing the inhabitants of Paradise: تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ ("You will recognize in their faces the radiance of bliss").

These faces appear such that they are "well-pleased with their [past] effort" (لِسَعْيِها راضِيَةٌ). Unlike the inhabitants of Hell, who gained nothing from their struggles and efforts except exhaustion and pain—being "laboring and weary"—the inhabitants of Paradise see the results of their efforts in the best possible way and are completely satisfied and content.

These are efforts that, under the light of Allah's grace, have grown manifold—sometimes ten times, sometimes seven hundred times, and sometimes even more. At times, they have purchased an "accountless reward" through them: "Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without account" (إِنَّما يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ) (Az-Zumar: 10).

Then, elaborating on this matter, He says: "They are in a lofty Paradise" (فِي جَنَّةٍ عالِيَةٍ). The word "‘Āliyah" (lofty) may refer to "spatial height," meaning they are in the high levels of Paradise, or "lofty status." Commentators have mentioned both possibilities, but the second interpretation seems more appropriate, though combining both meanings is also possible.

Next, He describes another aspect of this Paradise, which is spiritual and psychological in nature, adding: "Wherein you will hear no unsuitable or vain talk" (لا تَسْمَعُ فِيها لاغِيَةً) 1. There will be no speech indicating hypocrisy, enmity, conflict, or malice and envy; no lies, no slander or defamation, no backbiting or harassment, and not even any vain or useless talk. How tranquil is an environment purified of all such speech! If we reflect deeply, a major part of the distress in worldly life stems from hearing such words, which disturb the peace of the soul and social order and ignite the fires of fitnah (strife).

After mentioning this spiritual blessing and the tranquility that reigns over the souls of the inhabitants of Paradise due to the absence of vain talk, He proceeds to describe some of the material blessings of Paradise, saying: "Within it is a running spring" (فِيها عَيْنٌ جارِيَةٌ). Although "‘Ayn" (spring) is indefinite here, and usually an indefinite noun refers to a single individual, by the context of other Quranic verses, it carries a generic meaning and includes various springs. This is similar to what we read in Adh-Dhariyat, verse 15: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ ("Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and springs"). Some have also said that in every palace of the inhabitants of Paradise, there is a running spring, and the singular use of "‘Ayn" here refers to that—a spring that flows in whichever direction they desire, without the need to dig a canal or build a bed.

Of course, the existence of multiple springs, besides adding to the beauty and freshness, has the benefit that each provides a specific drink, sweetening and perfuming the palates of the inhabitants of Paradise at all times with various types of pure wine (Sharāb-un-Tahūr).

After mentioning the springs, He turns to the couches of Paradise and says: "Within it are couches raised high" (فِيها سُرُرٌ مَرْفُوعَةٌ). "Surur" is the plural of "Sarīr," derived from the root "Surūr" (joy), meaning thrones or couches upon which people sit in gatherings of intimacy and joy 1.

1: Raghib's Mufradat, root s-r-r.

The height of these couches is so that the inhabitants of Paradise may have a commanding view of all the surrounding scenery and enjoy the sight. Ibn Abbas says: "These high couches are such that when their owners intend to sit on them, they humble themselves and lower down, and after they sit, they return to their original state!" It is also possible that describing these couches as "raised" (Marfū‘ah) refers to their preciousness; as some have said, they are made of gold and adorned with emeralds, pearls, and rubies. A combination of both interpretations is also possible.

Since using those refreshing springs and pure heavenly wines requires vessels, He adds in the next verse: "And cups put in place" (وَ أَكْوابٌ مَوْضُوعَةٌ). Whenever they desire, the cups are filled from the springs and placed before them; they drink fresh, are quenched, and take delight—a delight whose description is impossible for the inhabitants of this world. "Akwāb" is the plural of "Kūb," meaning a goblet or a vessel that lacks a handle.

It is necessary to note that the Quran uses various terms for the vessels of the pure wine of the inhabitants of Paradise. Here and in some other verses, the term "Akwāb" is used, while in other verses, the term "Abārīq" (plural of Ibrīq, meaning a vessel with a handle and a spout) or "Ka’s" (meaning a cup full of wine) is used. For example: يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ بِأَكْوابٍ وَ أَبارِيقَ وَ كَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ ("There will circulate among them young boys made eternal with vessels, pitchers, and a cup [of wine] from a flowing spring") (Al-Waqi‘ah: 17-18).

Turning to more details of the heavenly blessings, He adds: "And cushions set in rows" (وَ نَمارِقُ مَصْفُوفَةٌ). "Namāriq" is the plural of "Numruqah," meaning a small cushion or pillow upon which one leans 1. These are typically used during complete relaxation. The expression "set in rows" (Masfūfah) indicates their abundance and the specific order governing them. This expression shows that they form collective gatherings of intimacy. These gatherings, free from any vanity or futility, where they speak only of Allah's graces, His endless blessings, and salvation from the pains of the world and the punishment of the Hereafter, possess such grace and pleasure that nothing can equal them.

In the final verse under discussion, He refers to the luxurious carpets of Paradise, saying: "And rich carpets spread out" (وَ زَرابِيُّ مَبْثُوثَةٌ). "Zarābiyy" is the plural of "Zarībiyyah," meaning fine, thick-piled carpets that are both soft and comfortable, as well as precious and high-priced. It is evident that alongside these means of comfort and pleasure, there are other means present there, of which these are but a small sample.

In these verses, seven important heavenly blessings are mentioned, each more attractive and beautiful than the last. In short, Paradise is a place incomparable in every respect, free from any discomfort, conflict, or strife, with various colorful fruits, pleasant melodies, flowing springs, pure wines, worthy servants, peerless spouses, jeweled thrones, luxurious carpets, sincere friends, and attractive vessels and cups by the springs. In summary, it contains blessings that can neither be described by the limited words of this world nor perceived in the realm of imagination. All of these await the arrival of the believers who, through their righteous deeds, have earned permission to enter this center of Divine blessing.

Beyond these material pleasures are spiritual delights, and above all is "Liqā’ Allāh" (Meeting with Allah), the manifestations of the Beloved, and the graces of that Worshipped One. If one moment of that is attained, it surpasses all the material blessings of Paradise. As the poet says: "If I have access to the hem of Your union, What else could I request from my destiny?! If I spend one breath with the Friend in both worlds, The fruit of both worlds for me would be that single breath."

1: Sihah al-Lughah, root n-m-r-q.

Ayahs

أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَى ٱلْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ (١٧)
Do they not observe the camel, [to see] how it has been created? (17)
وَإِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ رُفِعَتْ (١٨)
and the sky, how it has been raised? (18)
وَإِلَى ٱلْجِبَالِ كَيْفَ نُصِبَتْ (١٩)
and the mountains, how they have been set? (19)
وَإِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَيْفَ سُطِحَتْ (٢٠)
and the earth, how it has been surfaced? (20)
فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُذَكِّرٌ (٢١)
So admonish—for you are only an admonisher, (21)
لَّسْتَ عَلَيْهِم بِمُصَيْطِرٍ (٢٢)
and not a taskmaster over them— (22)
إِلَّا مَن تَوَلَّىٰ وَكَفَرَ (٢٣)
except him who turns back and disbelieves. (23)
فَيُعَذِّبُهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَكْبَرَ (٢٤)
Him Allah will punish with the greatest punishment. (24)
إِنَّ إِلَيْنَآ إِيَابَهُمْ (٢٥)
Indeed to Us will be their return. (25)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا حِسَابَهُم (٢٦)
Then, indeed, their reckoning will lie with Us. (26)

Commentary

Look at the Camel, for It Is a Sign Itself!

In the preceding verses, there were extensive discussions regarding Paradise and its blessings. However, in the verses under discussion, the focus shifts to the primary key for attaining all those blessings, which is "Knowledge of Allah" (Ma'rifat Allah). By mentioning four examples of the manifestations of God's power and His wondrous creation, and by inviting man to study them, He shows the way to enter Paradise. Simultaneously, this is an allusion to the infinite power of God, which is the key to resolving the issue of the "Resurrection."

First, He says: "Do they not look at the camels, how they are created?" (أَ فَلا يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَى الْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ).

Commentators have much to say regarding why the creation of the "camel" is emphasized here before anything else. However, it is evident that the primary audience was the Arabs of Mecca, for whom the "camel" constituted everything in their lives, and they dealt with it day and night.

Beyond this, this animal possesses extraordinary characteristics that distinguish it from other animals, and it is truly a sign among the signs of Allah. These include:

  1. Some livestock are used only for their meat, others primarily for their milk; some are fit only for riding, and others for carrying loads. However, the camel is an animal that combines all these aspects: its meat is usable, its milk is beneficial, and it is used for both riding and carrying heavy loads.

  2. The camel is the strongest and most resilient of domestic animals. It carries heavy loads, and remarkably, while it is kneeling, a heavy burden is placed upon it, and with a single movement, it rises and stands on its feet, whereas other beasts of burden lack the strength for such a feat.

  3. The camel can remain thirsty for consecutive days (about a week to ten days) and possesses great endurance against hunger.

  4. The camel can travel long distances every day, passing through difficult terrain and sandy deserts that no other animal is capable of crossing. For this reason, the Arabs call it the "Ship of the Desert"!

  5. It is very low-maintenance regarding nutrition and eats all kinds of thorns and dry brush.

  6. In unfavorable atmospheric conditions, amidst desert storms that blind and deafen, it resists using special mechanisms that Allah has created in its eyelids, ears, and nose, and continues on its way!

  7. Despite all its strength, the camel is among the most submissive animals, such that a small child can hold the reins of a train of camels and lead them wherever he wishes.

In short, the characteristics of this animal are such that reflecting upon its creation directs man toward the Great Creator who fashioned such a being. Indeed, the Quran asks: do those lost in the wilderness of negligence not ponder the wondrous secrets of this creature, so they might find a path to the Truth and return from their straying?

It goes without saying that the meaning of "look" (Nazar) in the phrase "أَ فَلا يَنْظُرُونَ" is not a casual glance, but a look accompanied by reflection, thought, and precision.

Next, He turns to the sky and says: "And at the sky, how it is raised high?" (وَ إِلَى السَّماءِ كَيْفَ رُفِعَتْ).

The sky with its grandeur, and all its wonders—the stars, the galaxies, and all that beauty and majesty that drowns man in wonder. Man sees himself as small and insignificant—indeed, like a "zero" against "infinity"—before the Creator of this vast universe, which is filled with order and calculation.

How are these massive celestial bodies each fixed in their orbits? And how are they positioned in their places without any pillars? Millions of years pass for the planets of the solar system, yet the primary axes of their movement do not change.

Although the creation of the sky has always been wondrous, in the light of today's scientific discoveries, its wonders have become far greater and its grandeur more manifest.

Should one not reflect upon the Creator and Disposer of this great universe and draw closer to His great and lofty purposes?

Then, descending from the sky to the "earth," He adds: "And at the mountains, how they are fixed?" (وَ إِلَى الْجِبالِ كَيْفَ نُصِبَتْ).

Mountains whose roots are interconnected, surrounding the earth like links of armor, reducing the tremors caused by internal molten materials and the tides caused by the gravitational pull of the moon and sun to a minimum.

Mountains that are secure shelters and shields against storms; had they not existed, the globe would have turned into an uninhabitable desert.

And finally, mountains that preserve water within themselves and gradually release it toward thirsty lands, creating the vitality of life, greenery, and freshness on their slopes. Perhaps it is for these reasons that in other verses of the Quran, mountains are introduced as the "pegs" (Awtad) of the earth.

Fundamentally, mountains are symbols of awe, greatness, and solidity, and everywhere they are a source of goodness and blessing. Perhaps for this reason, man finds a more awakened mind in the heart of the mountains; it is not without reason that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, before his mission, spent long periods engaged in worship at "Jabal al-Nur" and the "Cave of Hira."

"Nusibat" is derived from the root "Nasb," meaning to fix firmly. This expression may also be an incidental allusion to the nature of the mountains' formation at the beginning of creation—the very thing that modern science has unveiled, attributing the emergence of mountains to various factors and identifying several types:

  • Mountains formed by the folding of the earth's crust.
  • Mountains formed by volcanoes.
  • Mountains resulting from erosion caused by rain.
  • Mountains formed in the heart of the seas, consisting of marine sediments and the remains of animals (such as coral mountains and islands).

Indeed, each of these mountains, their formation, and their effects and blessings are worthy of precision and importance, and for awakened humans, they are living signs of the Lord's power.

Then He turns to the earth and says: "And at the earth, how it is spread out?" (وَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ كَيْفَ سُطِحَتْ).

How have continuous rains washed the mountains and created soil particles, then spread them in the hollows, providing man with flat lands ready for agriculture and suitable for any kind of construction?

If the globe were truly all mountains and valleys, how difficult and exhausting would living upon it be? Who leveled it and made it usable before our birth?

These are all matters that the Quran invites us to reflect upon.

Here, the question arises: what is the connection and link between these four matters: the camel, the sky, the mountains, and the earth?

Fakhr al-Razi says in his commentary: "This is because the Quran was revealed in the Arabic language, and they [the Arabs] frequently undertook journeys because their lands were devoid of agriculture and produce. On the other hand, most of their travels were conducted by camel. When they traveled in these terrifying deserts, far from society, reflection and thought would come alive in them. There was no one to speak with them, and nothing to occupy their eyes and ears. In this state, when they reflected, the first thing their eyes fell upon was the camel they were riding; they would see its wondrous sight and fall into thought. When they looked above their heads, they saw nothing but the sky. When they looked to their left and right, nothing was seen but mountains. And when they looked beneath their feet, there was nothing but the earth. It is as if Allah intends to command them to reflect—a reflection during solitude that naturally revolves around these four things." [1]

However, if we wish to move our gaze beyond the limited living environment of the Arab and reflect in a broader atmosphere, it can be said: the four matters mentioned in the above verses constitute the infrastructure of human life:

The sky is the center of light, rain, and air; the earth is the center for growing all kinds of food; the mountains are the symbol of stability and the reservoir of water and minerals; and the camel is a clear example of the domestic animals placed at man's disposal.

In this way, agricultural, pastoral, and industrial matters are all hidden within these four things. Reflection upon these various blessings inevitably compels man to thank the Benefactor, and thanking the Benefactor invites him to the Knowledge of Allah and the recognition of the Creator of the blessings.

Following this monotheistic discussion, He addresses the Prophet, saying: "So remind them, for you are only a reminder." (فَذَكِّرْ إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُذَكِّرٌ).

"You are not a dictator over them [to force them into faith]." (لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ).

Indeed, the creation of the sky, the earth, the mountains, and the animals shows that this world is not without calculation, and the creation of man also had a purpose. Now that this is the case, acquaint them with the goals of creation through your reminders, show them the way to proximity to God, and be their leader and guide on the path of perfection.

Of course, the path of perfection is traversed only when it is accompanied by desire, will, and choice; otherwise, "compulsory perfection" is a meaningless term. You can never force them, and even if you could, it would be of no benefit.

Some have imagined that this command was before the revelation of the command for "Jihad" and was abrogated by the ruling of Jihad.

What a great mistake! The matter of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله reminding and preaching began from the very first day and continued until the last minute of his life, and after him, it has continued and will continue through the Infallible Successors and the scholars of religion. This is not something that can be abrogated. Likewise, not forcing people into faith is a fixed principle; the primary goal of Jihad is to fight against tyrants and remove obstacles from the path of truth-seeking people.

This is similar to what appears in Surah An-Nisa, verse 80, which says: "وَ مَنْ تَوَلَّى فَما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظاً" (And whoever turns away—We have not sent you over them as a guardian), and verse 107 of Al-An'am, and 48 of Ash-Shura, which express the same meaning.

"Musaytir" is derived from the root "Satr," meaning the lines of a book. A "Musaytir" is one who arranges lines and organizes the rows of a book. Subsequently, it was applied to any person who dominates something and organizes its lines, or forces someone to perform an action by compulsion.

In the next verse, as an exception, He says: "Except him who turns away and disbelieves." (إِلَّا مَنْ تَوَلَّى وَ كَفَرَ).

"Then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment." (فَيُعَذِّبُهُ اللَّهُ الْعَذابَ الْأَكْبَرَ).

Regarding which phrase this exception refers to, there are various interpretations:

First: It is an exception from the object of the phrase "fadhakkir" (so remind). That is, there is no need to remind the stubborn individuals who turn away from the truth and are unreceptive to advice. In fact, it is similar to what appears in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 83: "فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَ يَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ" (So leave them to converse vainly and amuse themselves until they meet their Day which they are promised)!

Second: It is from a deleted phrase, and the meaning is: "Remind, for reminding is beneficial for all, except those who harbor enmity toward the truth," similar to what is in Surah Al-A'la, verse 9: "فَذَكِّرْ إِنْ نَفَعَتِ الذِّكْرى" (So remind, if the reminder should benefit—assuming the verse has a conditional meaning).

Third: It is an exception from the pronoun "them" ('alayhim) in the previous verse, meaning: "You have no authority over them, except those who turn away and act out of obstinacy and defiance, whom you are duty-bound to confront." [1]

All these apply if the exception is "connected" (muttasil). However, the possibility also exists that it is a "disconnected" (munqati') exception, which carries the sense of "but." The meaning of the sentence would then be: "But those who turn away and disbelieve, Allah has authority over them," or "Allah will punish them with the greatest punishment."

Among these interpretations, two are more suitable: first, that the exception is connected and returns to the phrase "لَسْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِمُصَيْطِرٍ," referring to the use of force against oppressors; or that it is disconnected and refers to the inclusion of divine punishment for the stubborn disbelievers.

The "greatest punishment" refers to the "punishment of the Hereafter" as opposed to the "punishment of this world," which is small and insignificant in comparison. As we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 26: "فَأَذاقَهُمُ اللَّهُ الْخِزْيَ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ لَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ" (So Allah made them taste disgrace in worldly life. But the punishment of the Hereafter is greater, if they only knew).

It is also possible that "the greatest punishment" refers to a more severe portion of the punishment of Resurrection and Hell, for the punishment of all criminals in Hell is not equal.

At the end of this Surah, He says in a threatening tone: "Indeed, to Us is their return." (إِنَّ إِلَيْنا إِيابَهُمْ).

Then He adds: "Then indeed, upon Us is their account." (ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا حِسابَهُمْ).

This is, in fact, a kind of comfort and consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, so that he does not become distressed or discouraged by their obstinacy and continues his work. Simultaneously, it is a threat to all these stubborn disbelievers so they may know with whom their account lies.

Thus, Surah Al-Ghashiyah, which began with the issue of the Resurrection, also ends with the issue of the Resurrection. In between, it alludes to Monotheism and Prophethood, which constitute the foundations of the Resurrection. Within the opening verses of this Surah, a portion of the heavy punishments for criminals was mentioned, followed by a significant part of the soul-nourishing rewards for the believers. Meanwhile, the choice of path is left to the people, yet they are warned that the return of all is to Allah and their account is with Him.

Furthermore, it clarifies that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is commissioned to convey the message and is not responsible for the disbelief, error, or sin of the people. Such is the duty of all callers to the path of Truth.

O Allah! On that day when the return of all creation is to You and the accounting of all is upon You, include us in Your grace and mercy. O Lord! Deliver us from Your greatest punishment through Your greatest mercy. O God! Your heavenly blessings, a glimpse of which You have recounted in this Surah, are very precious and longing-inducing. If we do not deserve them by our deeds, grant them to us by Your grace.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 26, p. 436

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Ghashiyah, 3rd of Ramadan al-Mubarak, 1407 AH.

[^1]: From a Hadith narrated in "Al-Durr al-Manthur" from the Noble Prophet (ص), it is understood that the Prophet (ص) was duty-bound to fight against idolaters, and otherwise, his only duty was to remind.
